question: If a bar magnet of moment ${10^{ - 4}}A{m^2}$ is kept in a uniform magnetic field of $12 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,T$ such that it makes an angle of $30^\circ $ with the direction of magnetic field, then the torque acting on the magnet is
explanation:
Given,
Magnetic moment of bar magnet,
$M = {10^{ - 4}}A{m^2}$
Magnetic field, $B=12 \times 10^{-3} T$
Torque on the bar magnet $\tau=M B \sin \theta$
$\Rightarrow \tau=10^{-4} \times 12 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{1}{2}$
$ = 6 \times {10^{ - 7}}Nm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Torque on a Magnet Placed in Uniform Magnetic Field

question: A short bar magnet placed in a uniform magnetic field making an angle with the field experiences a torque. If the angle made by the magnet with field is changed from $30^\circ $ to $45^\circ $, the torque of the magnet
explanation:
The magnitude of torque on a bar magnet in magnetic field is $\tau=M B \sin \theta \Rightarrow \tau \alpha \sin \theta$
$\therefore \dfrac{\tau_{2}}{\tau_{1}}=\dfrac{\sin \theta_{2}}{\sin \theta_{1}}=\dfrac{\sin 45^{\circ}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\dfrac{1 / \sqrt{2}}{1 / 2}=\sqrt{2}$
$\therefore \quad \tau_{2}=1.414 \tau_{1}$
$\therefore \quad \%$ change in $\tau$,
$\Delta \tau \%=\dfrac{\tau_{2}-\tau_{1}}{\tau_{1}} \times 100=\dfrac{1.414 \tau_{1}-\tau_{1}}{\tau_{1}} \times 100$
$=41.4 \%$ increase.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Torque on a Magnet Placed in Uniform Magnetic Field

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
A magnetic dipole in a uniform magnetic field experiences a torque but no net force.
A magnetic dipole aligns itself with the magnetic field to minimize its potential energy.
explanation: Statement A is true because a magnetic dipole in a uniform magnetic field experience a torque, which tends to align it with the field, but there is no net force acting on the dipole. Statement B is also true because when a  dipole aligns itself with the field, its potential energy is minimized, which is the most stable figuration. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Torque on a Magnet Placed in Uniform Magnetic Field

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The torque on a magnetic dipole in a uniform magnetic field is maximum when the dipole is aligned with the field.
The magnetic potential energy is minimum when the magnetic dipole is perpendicular to the magnetic field.
explanation: Statement A is false because the torque on a magnetic dipole in a uniform magnetic field is actually zero when the dipole is aligned with the field, as there is no angle to create a torque (torque $=m B \sin \theta$, and $\sin 0^{\circ}=0$). The maximum torque occurs when the dipole is perpendicular to the field $\left(\sin 90^{\circ}=1\right)$.     Statement B is also false because the magnetic potential energy is minimum when the magnetic dipole is parallel to the magnetic field ($\theta=0^{\circ}$), not perpendicular.     At  $\theta=90^{\circ}$, which is when the dipole is perpendicular to the field, the potential energy can be set to zero by choosing the constant of integration accordingly.   Correct option is (4)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics

question: A bar magnet of magnetic moment $2 A-m^{2}$ lies aligned with the direction of a uniform magnetic field of 0.3 $T$. The amount of work required by an external torque to turn the magnet so as to align its magnetic moment normal to the field direction is
explanation:
Given,
Magnetic moment, $M=2 {Am}^{2}$
Magnetic field, $B=0.3 T$
Work done by external torque to rotate the bar magnet by $90^{\circ}$
$W=-M B\left(\cos \theta_{2}-\cos \theta_{1}\right)$
$W=-M B\left[\cos 90^{\circ}-\cos 0^{\circ}\right]$
$W=-2 \times 0.3 \times-1 \Rightarrow W=0.6 J$
Hence work done is $0.6{\text{ }}J.$
Hence, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A short bar magnet placed with its axis at $30^{\circ}$ with an external field of $800 G$ experiences a torque of  $0.016{\text{ }}Nm.$  The magnetic moment of the bar magnet is
explanation:
$\because \quad \tau=M B \sin \theta$ where, $\tau=$ torque experienced by bar magnet
$M=$ magnetic moment of bar magnet
$B=$ external magnetic field intensity
$\theta=$ angle made by the axis of bar magnet with the external magnetic field
Given $\tau=0.016, B=800 G=800 \times 10^{-4} T$
and $\theta=30^{\circ}$
So, $0.016=M \times 8 \times 10^{-2} \times \sin \left(30^{\circ}\right)$
$\Rightarrow 0.016=M \times 8 \times 10^{-2} \times \dfrac{1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow 1.6 \times 10^{-2}=4 M \times 10^{-2}$
$\Rightarrow M=\dfrac{1.6}{4} \Rightarrow M=0.4 A m^{2}$
Hence, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Torque on a Magnet Placed in Uniform Magnetic Field

question: A small bar magnet experiences a torque of  $0.016{\text{ }}Nm$  when placed with its axis at $30^{\circ}$ with an external field of $0.04 T$. If the bar magnet is replaced by a solenoid of cross - sectional area of $1 {~cm}^{2}$ and 1000 turns but having the same magnetic moment as that of bar magnet, then the current flowing through  the solenoid is
explanation:
Given, torque on a bar magnet, $\tau=0.016 {Nm}$. $\theta=30^{\circ}$ and magnetic field, $B=0.04 T$
Since, $\tau=M B \sin \theta$
$\Rightarrow 0.016=M \times 0.04 \times \sin 30^{\circ}$
$ \Rightarrow M = \frac{{2 \times 0.016}}{{0.04}} \Rightarrow m = 0.8A{m^2}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
For solenoid, area of cross-section,
$A=1 {~cm}^{2}=10^{-4} {~m}^{2}$
Number of turns in solenoid, $N=1000$
$\therefore$ magnetic moment of solenoid,
$M=N I A$
$\Rightarrow \quad I=\dfrac{M}{N A}=\dfrac{0.8}{1000 \times 10^{-4}}=8 {~A}$ [From Eq. (1)]
Hence, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Torque on a Magnet Placed in Uniform Magnetic Field

question: A magnetic dipole is under the influence of two orthogonal magnetic fields, $B_{1}=0.5 \times 10^{-3} T$ and $B_{2}=0.866 \times 10^{-3} T$. If the dipole comes to stable equilibrium at an angle $\theta$ with respect to $B_{2}$ field, then the value of $\theta$ is
explanation:
From tangent law, we have,
$M B_{2} \sin \theta=M B_{1} \sin \left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952731/preptracker/questions/q_111096_explanation_1764952730924.png
$\Rightarrow \tan \theta=\dfrac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=\dfrac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.866 \times 10^{-3}}=\dfrac{1 / 2}{\sqrt{3} / 2}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
$\tan \theta=\tan 30^{\circ} \Rightarrow \theta=30^{\circ}$
Hence, the correct option is (2).

question: The work done in turning a magnet of magnetic moment $M$ by an angle of $90^{\circ}$ from the meridian, is $n$ times the corresponding work done to turn it through an angle of $30^{\circ}$. The value of ' $n$ ' is
explanation:

for turning $90^{\circ}$,
${W}_{1}={MB}\left(\cos 0^{\circ}-\cos 90^{\circ}\right)={MB}$
for turning $30^{\circ}$,
${W}_{2}={MB}\left(\cos 0^{\circ}-\cos 30^{\circ}\right)=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} {MB}$

question: The magnetic moment of a bar magnet is $0.5\,A{m^2}.$  It is suspended in a uniform magnetic field of  $8 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,T.$  The work done in rotating it from its most stable to most unstable position is
explanation:
Here : $M = 0.5A{m^2},B = 8 \times {10^{ - 2}}T$
For stable equilibrium, $\theta=0^{\circ}$
For unstable, equilibrium, $\theta=180^{\circ}$
$W=\Delta U=U_{f}-U_{i}=$
$-M B \cos 180^{\circ}-\left(-M B \cos 0^{\circ}\right)$
$W=2 M B=2 \times 0.5 \times 8 \times 10^{-2}=8 \times 10^{-2} J$

question: Two identical magnets with a length $10\,cm$ and weight $50\,g$ for each are arranged freely with their like poles facing in a vertical glass tube as shown in figure. The upper magnet in the air above the lower one so that the distance between the nearest poles of the magnets is $3\,mm.$ Determine the pole strength of the poles of these magnets.
explanation:
Since lengths of both the magnets are quite large as compared to separation between them, the interaction is effective only between their $N$ -poles.
Let ${m}$ be the strength of each pole of the two magnets. Then,
${\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{m \times m}{r^{2}}=}$ weight of the magnet
Here, weight of the magnet $ = 50\,gf = 50 \times {10^{ - 3}}kgf$
$=50 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.8 {~N}$
and ${r=3 {~mm}=3 \times 10^{-3} {~m}}$
${\because \quad \dfrac{10^{-7} \times m^{2}}{\left(3 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}}=50 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.8}$
or $m = 6.64\,Am$

question: Two equal magnetic poles placed $5\,cm$ in air attract each other with a force of ${14.4 \times 10^{-4} {~N}}$. How far   from each other should they be placed so that the force of attraction will be ${1.6 \times 10^{-4} {~N}}$ ?
explanation:
Let ${m}$ be the pole strength of each pole.
In first case: ${F=14.4 \times 10^{-4} {~N} ; r=5 {~cm}=0.05 {~m}}$
Now, ${\quad F=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{m \times m}{r^{2}}}$
or ${\quad 14.4 \times 10^{-4}=10^{-7} \times \dfrac{m^{2}}{(0.05)^{2}}}$
or ${\quad m= \pm 6 {Am}}$
In second case: Let ${r^{\prime}}$ be the distance between the poles, when force of attraction becomes ${F^{\prime}=1.6 \times 10^{-4} {~N}}$.
Now, ${\quad F^{\prime}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{m \times m}{r^{\prime 2}}}$
or $1.6 \times 10^{-4}=10^{-7} \times \dfrac{6 \times 6}{r^{\prime} 2}$
or $r^{\prime}=0.15 {~m}$
subject: Physics

question: A magnet of moment ${2 {~A} {~m}^{2}}$ is placed in a uniform magnetic field of ${5 {~Wb} / {m}^{2}}$. If the magnet experiences a torque of 5 $N\,m$ , then the angle between the direction of magnetic field and magnet is
explanation:
Magnetic moment, ${m=2 {~A} {~m}^{2}}$
Torque acting, ${\tau=5 {~N} {~m}}$
${\therefore \quad \tau=N I A B \sin \theta}$
${\sin \theta=\dfrac{\tau}{N I A B}=\dfrac{5}{m B}=\dfrac{5}{2 \times 5}=0.5}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left[ {\because m = NIA} \right]$
${\sin \theta=\dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \theta=\sin ^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) \quad \therefore \theta=\dfrac{\pi}{6}}$.
So correct option is (1)

question: If the work done in turning a magnet of magnetic moment $M$ by an angle of $90^{\circ}$ from the magnetic meridian is $n$ times the corresponding work done to turn it through an angle of $60^{\circ}$, then the value of $n$ is
explanation:
The work done in turning a magnet from position $\theta_{1}$ to $\theta_{2}$ in a magnetic field $B$ is
$W=M B\left(\cos \theta_{1}-\cos \theta_{2}\right)
$
As per question
$W_{1}=M B\left(\cos 0^{\circ}-\cos 90^{\circ}\right)$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,$$=M B(1-0)=M B$
and $W_{2}=M B\left(\cos 0^{\circ}-\cos 60^{\circ}\right)$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,$$=M B\left(1-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)=\dfrac{1}{2} M B$
As $W_{1}=n W_{2}$ (given)
$\therefore \quad n = \frac{{{W_1}}}{{{W_2}}} = \frac{{MB}}{{\frac{1}{2}MB}} = 2$
subject: Physics

question: A magnetic needle is placed on a cork floating in a still lake in the northern hemisphere. Does the needle together with the cork move towards the north of the lake?
explanation: Magnetic needle is a dipole which is in the earth's uniform magnetic field and as a dipole in a uniform field, it does not experience any net force but may experience a couple. So the needle together with the cork will not translate, i.e. not move towards the north of the lake, but will rotate and set itself parallel to the field with its north pole pointing north.

question: A torque of ${10^{ - 5}}\,N - m$ is required to hold a magnet at $90^{\circ}$ with the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field. The torque required to hold it at $30^{\circ}$ will be
explanation:
The magnet in a magnetic field
experiences a torque which rotates the
magnet to a position in which the axis
of the magnet is parallel to the field.
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\tau = MB\sin \theta $
where, $M$ is the magnetic dipole
moment, $B$ is the magnetic field and $\theta$
is the angle between the two.
Given, ${\tau _1} = {10^{ - 5}}\,N - m,$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\theta _1} = {\text{ }}90^\circ {\text{ and}}{\theta _2} = 30^\circ .$
As we know,
${\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\tau _1} = MB\sin 90^\circ {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\tau _2} = MB\sin 30^\circ \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
On dividing Eq. (1) by Eq. (2), we get
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{{{\tau _1}}}{{{\tau _2}}} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{{\tau _2}}} = \frac{1}{{1/2}}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,{\tau _2} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 5}}}}{2} = \frac{{10}}{2} \times {10^{ - 6}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = 5 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,N - m$
subject: Physics

question: A bar magnet with a magnetic moment $5.0\,A{m^2}$ is placed in parallel position relative to a magnetic field of $0.4\,T.$ The amount of required work done in turning the magnet from parallel to antiparallel position relative to the field direction is
explanation:

question: Force between two identical short bar magnets whose centres are $r$ metre apart is 8.1$N$, when their axes are along the same line. If separation is incresed to 3$r$ and the axis are rearranged perpendicularly, the force between them would become
explanation:

when axes are $\perp$
$F_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{3 M_{1} M_{2}}{(3 r)^{4}}$
Therefore, $\frac{{{F_1}}}{{{F_2}}} = \frac{{6{{(3)}^4}}}{3} \Rightarrow {F_2} = 0.05\,N$

question: A torque of ${10^{ - 5}}Nm$ is required to hold a magnet at with the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field. The torque required to hold it at $30^{\circ}$ will be:
explanation:
The magnet in a magnetic field experiences a torque which rotates to a position in which the axis of the magnet is parallel to the field.
$\tau=M B \sin \theta$
Where, $M$ is magnetic dipole moment, $B$ the magnetic field and $\theta$ the angle between the two.
Given, ${\tau _1} = {10^{ - 5}}Nm,{\theta _1} = {90^ \circ }$ and $\theta_{2}=30^{\circ}$
${\tau _1} = MB\sin 90^\circ \,\,\,\,(1)$
${\tau _2} = MB\sin 30^\circ \,\,\,(2)$
On dividing Eq. (1) by Eq. (2), we get
$\frac{{{\tau _1}}}{{{\tau _2}}} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{{\tau _2}}} = \frac{1}{{1/2}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\tau _2} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 5}}}}{2} = \frac{{10}}{2} \times {10^{ - 6}} = 5 \times {10^{ - 6}}Nm$

question: A bar magnet has coercivity $4 \times 10^{3} {~A} / {m}$. It is placed inside a solenoid of  $12\,cm$  length and 60 turns. The current that should be passed through the solenoid to demagnetise, the bar magnet is
explanation:

$n=\dfrac{N}{L}=\dfrac{60}{12 \times 10^{-2}}=500$ turns $/ {m}$

(iii) High permeability.

The magnetisation of steel is not easily destroyed by stray fields. Once magnetised under a strong field, it retains magnetisation for a long duration.
Hence, the correct option is (4).

question:
Assertion: Diamagnetic materials can exhibit magnetism.

Reason: Diamagnetic materials have permanent magnetic dipole moment .
explanation:
Diamagnetic materials are typically repelled by magnetic fields. When placed in a magnetic field, they exhibit a weak form of magnetism called diamagnetism. Diamagnetic materials have no permanent magnetic dipole moment; instead, they develop induced magnetic moments in the opposite direction to the applied magnetic field. These induced moments are quite weak, and they do not result in a net attraction to the magnetic field. Therefore, diamagnetic materials do not exhibit magnetism in the same way that ferromagnetic or paramagnetic materials do.
Diamagnetic materials do not have a permanent magnetic dipole moment. Instead, they develop temporary, induced magnetic moments when placed in an external magnetic field. These induced moments are opposite in direction to the applied field and are a result of the rearrangement of electron orbits within the atoms or molecules of the material. When the external magnetic field is removed, the induced moments vanish. Diamagnetic materials do not retain any permanent magnetism.
The reason provided is not accurate, as it incorrectly states that diamagnetic materials have a permanent magnetic dipole moment. In reality, diamagnetic materials only exhibit weak, temporary diamagnetism when subjected to an external magnetic field. So, correct option is (3)

When a paramagnetic sample is cooled, the thermal energy of the atoms or molecules decreases, which reduces the random thermal motion. As a result, the magnetic dipoles in the material are more likely to align with the external magnetic field, leading to a greater magnetization of the sample at lower temperatures. This is a well-known property of paramagnetic materials, and cooling them can indeed enhance their magnetization.
In the case of paramagnetic materials, magnetization does depend on temperature. As explained in the assertion, lowering the temperature can lead to an increase in magnetization for paramagnetic substances. Therefore, the reason provided is not accurate and does not correctly explain the behavior of paramagnetic materials.
The correct answer is (3) Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect because the reason contradicts the behavior of paramagnetic materials, which do exhibit temperaturedependent magnetization.
So, correct option is (3)

question: The magnetic field intensity ($H$) at the centre of a long solenoid carrying a current of $2 A$, is found to be $1000 {~A} / {m}$. The number of turns per centimetre of the solenoid is (Take, $\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} {Tm} / {A}$)
explanation:
Given, magnetic field intensity, $H=1000 {~A} / {m}$
Current through solenoid, $I=2 A$
Let number of turns per unit length be $n$ As we know that,
$B=\mu_{0} H$ and $B=n \mu_{0} I$
where, $B=$ magnetic field.
$\Rightarrow \mu_{0} H=n \mu_{0} I$
$\Rightarrow n=\dfrac{H}{I}=\dfrac{1000}{2}=500$ turns $/ {m}$
$=\dfrac{500}{1 {~m}}=\dfrac{500}{100 {~cm}}=5$ turns $/ {cm}$
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics

question: The susceptibility of Al is $2 \times 10^{-5}$. The percent increase in the magnetic field when the space within a current carrying  toroid  to filled with $A l$ is
explanation:
By using the net magnetic field in the toroid filled with $A l$.
$B_{0}=\mu_{0} H$ before toroid is filled with $A l$
$B=\mu H$ after toroid is filled with $A l$.
Percentage increase in magnetic field
$=\dfrac{B-B_{0}}{B_{0}} \times 100=\left[\dfrac{\mu_{-}-\mu_{0}}{\mu_{0}}\right] \times 100$
$=\left[\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{\mu_{0}}-1\right] \times 100$
$=\left[\mu_{r}-1\right] \times 100=\chi_{m} \times 100$
$=2 \times 10^{-5} \times 100=2 \times 10^{-3}$
Hence, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: An iron bar having a cross-sectional area of $2 \times 10^{-5} {~m}^{2}$ and magnetising field of $2400 {~A} / {m}$ produce a magnetic flux $2.4 \pi \times 10^{-5} {~Wb}$. What will be the value of permeability $\mu$ and susceptibility $\chi_{m}$ of  the bar ?     (given, $\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} {H} / {m}$)
explanation:
Given, cross-sectional area of iron bar,
$A=2 \times 10^{-5} {~m}^{2}$
Magnetising field, $H=2400 {~A} / {m}$
Magnetic flux, $\phi=2.4 \pi \times 10^{-5} {~Wb}$
We know that, $\phi=B A \Rightarrow B=\dfrac{\phi}{A}$
$=\dfrac{2.4 \pi \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-5}}=1.2 \pi T$
$\therefore$ Again, $B=\mu H$
$\Rightarrow \mu=\dfrac{B}{H}=\dfrac{1.2 \pi}{2400} \Rightarrow \mu=5 \pi \times 10^{-4} {H} / {m}$
Again, we know that, magnetic susceptibility
$\chi_{m}=\mu_{r}-1=\dfrac{\mu}{\mu_{0}}-1$
$=\dfrac{5 \pi \times 10^{-4}}{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}-1=1.25 \times 10^{3}-1$
$=1250-1=1249$
Hence, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A solenoid has a core of a material with relative permeability 501.   The windings of the solenoid are insulated from the core and carry a current of  $2.5{\text{ }}A.$  If the number of turns are 900 per metre, then the magnetisation (in $A / m$ ) is
explanation:
Given, $\mu_{r}=501$
Current through solenoid, $I=2.5 {~A}$ and number of turns per unit length,
$n=\dfrac{N}{l}=900$
As we know that, $B=\mu_{0} H$
$\Rightarrow H=\dfrac{B}{\mu_{0}}$
Since, magnetic field due to solenoid, $B=\mu_{0} n I$
Here, $\mu$ is permeability of medium
and $\mu=\mu_{r} \mu_{0}$
where, $\mu_{r}$ is relative permeability.
$\therefore H=\dfrac{B}{\mu_{0}}=\dfrac{n \mu I}{\mu_{0}}=\dfrac{n \mu_{r} \mu_{0} I}{\mu_{0}}$
$ \Rightarrow H = n{\mu _r}I = 900 \times 501 \times 2.5$ $ = 1.12 \times {10^6}\,A/m$
Hence, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: The susceptibility of magnesium at $27^\circ C$ is $1.2 \times 10^{5}$. At what temperature (in $K$ ) will the susceptibility equal to $1.44 \times 10^{5}$ ?
explanation:
According to Curie law,
${\chi _m} = \frac{C}{T}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{{{\chi _m}}}{{{{\chi '}_m}}} = \frac{{T'}}{T}$
$ \Rightarrow T' = \frac{{{\chi _m}}}{{\chi _m^\prime }} \times T$
$ = \frac{{300 \times 1.2 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{1.44 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,[27^\circ C\, = 300K]$
$\therefore \quad T' = 250\,K$

$=\mu_{0}(1+\chi) B\left[\because \mu=\mu_{0}(1+\chi)\right]$
Where, $B=$ magnetic field in air
$\mu_{0}=$ permeability of vaccum
$\chi=$ magnetic susceptibility
As we know that magnetic field inside the solenoid is given by
$B=\mu_{0} n I$
So, $B^{\prime}=\mu_{0}(1+\chi) n I$

question: The magnetization of bar magnet of length 5$cm$, cross sectional area $2\,c{m^2}$ and net magnetic moment $1A{m^2}$ is
explanation:

$a = 2\,c{m^2} = 2 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,{m^2}$
and $M = 1A{m^{ - 2}}$
The magnetisation of the bar magnet is given by
$I=\dfrac{M}{V}$
Where, $V=$ volume of bar magnet $=a \times l$
$\Rightarrow I=\dfrac{M}{a \times l}$
Substituting given values, we get
$I = \frac{1}{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 4}} \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}} = \frac{1}{{{{10}^{ - 5}}}}$
$ = 1 \times {10^5}\,A/m$

question: Coercivity of a magnet where the ferromagnet gets completely demagnetized is $3 \times {10^3}A{m^{ - 1}}$. The minimum current required to be passed in a solenoid having 1000 turns per metre, so that the magnet gets completely demagnetized when placed inside the solenoid is
explanation:

question: An iron rod is placed parallel to magnetic field to intensity $2000A{m^{ - 1}}$. The magnetic flux through the rod is $6 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,Wb$ and its cross sectional area is $3\,c{m^2}$. The magentic permeability of the rod in $W b A^{-1} m^{-1}$ is
explanation:

question: Asolenoid has core of a material with relative permeability 500 and its windings carry a current of 1$A$. The number of turns of the solenoid is 500 per metre. The magnetisation of the material is nearly
explanation:

As $\mu_{r}=1+\chi$, where $\chi$ is the magnetic susceptibility of the material
$\text { or } \chi=\left(\mu_{r}-1\right)$
Magnetisation $ = \chi H = ({\mu _r} - 1)H$
$ = (500 - 1) \times 500A{m^{ - 1}} = 499 \times 500A{m^{ - 1}}$

question: A domain in a ferromagnetic substances is in the form of a cube of side length $1 \mu \mathrm{m}$. If it contains $8 \times 10^{10}$ atoms and each atomic dipole has a dipole moment of $9 \times {10^{ - 24}}A{m^2}$, then magnetisation of the domain is
explanation:

question: The magnetic susceptibility of a paramagnetic material at $ - 73^\circ C$ is 0.002 , its value at $ - 223^\circ C$ will be
explanation:

${T_2} = - 223 + 273 = 50\,K$
$\Rightarrow \chi_{2}=\dfrac{200}{50} \times \chi_{1}=\dfrac{200}{50} \times 0.002=0.008$

question: Match the terms in Column I with their corresponding descriptions in Column II related to Gauss's laws for magnetism and electrostatics, and the concept of magnetic monopoles and choose the correct option from codes given below.
explanation: Gauss's law for magnetism (A) states that the net magnetic flux through any closed surface is zero (Q). For magnetic fields, the number of field lines leaving a closed surface (B) is always balanced by the number of lines entering it (P), ensuring no net flux. In contrast, Gauss's law for electrostatics (C) is related to the net electric charge enclosed by the surface  (S). The non-existence of magnetic monopoles (D) is supported by the absence of sources or sinks of magnetic field B (R) meaning magnetic field lines do not begin or end but form continuous loops. Correct option is (2)

Assertion: Earth's magnetic field inside a closed iron box is less as compared to the outside.

Reason: The magnetic permeability of iron is low.
explanation: The relative magnetic permeability $\left(\mu_{r}\right)$ of iron is actually high, not low. As a result, magnetic lines of force prefer to pass through or concentrate in the iron walls of the box. This concentration of magnetic lines within the iron material effectively shields the interior from the external magnetic field, causing the magnetic field inside the closed iron box to be weaker than the field outside. So correct option is (3).

question:
Assertion: A disc-shaped magnet is levitated above a superconducting material that has been cooled by liquid nitrogen.

Reason: Superconductors repel a magnet.
explanation: Super conductors are diamagnetic below $T_{C}$ (Here $T_{C}$ means temperature at which normal conductor becomes super conductor). When superconductors reach their critical temperature $\left(T_{C}\right)$, the Meissner effect expels magnetic fields, causing magnetic repulsion. This repulsion, seen when a magnet hoveres above a cooled superconductor, is due to the induced magnetic field opposing the applied field. So correct option is (1).

question:
Consider the statements given below.
I. Isolated magnetic poles do not exist.
II. There are no sources or sinks of $B$, the simplest magnetic element is a dipole or a current loop.
III. All magnetic phenomena can be explained in terms of an arrangement of dipoles and/or current loops.
IV. The net magnetic flux through any closed surface is zero.

explanation:
Isolated magnetic poles (also called monopoles) are not known to exist.
There are no sources or sinks of $B$; the simplest magnetic element is a dipole or a current loop. All magnetic phenomena can be explained in terms of an arrangement of dipole and/or current loops. Thus, Gauss's law for magnetism is 'The net magnetic flux through any closed surface is zero'.

question: A planet has magnetic dipole moment of $27 \times 10^{22} A-m^{2}$. If the radius of the planet is  $300{\rm{ }}km,$  what would be the magnetic field at its equator ?  $\left. {\left[ {\frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }} = {{10}^{ - 7}}} \right.\,{\text{in}}\,\,{\text{SI}}\,\,{\text{units}}} \right]$
explanation:
Magnetic dipole moment of planet,
$M=27 \times 10^{22} A-m^{2}$
Radius of planet, $R=300 {~km}=3 \times 10^{5} {~m}$
$\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}=10^{-7}$ ( in SI units )
Magnetic field at the equator,
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{M}{R^{3}}=10^{-7} \times \dfrac{27 \times 10^{22}}{\left(3 \times 10^{5}\right)^{3}}$
$=10^{-7} \times \dfrac{27 \times 10^{22}}{27 \times 10^{15}}=1 {~T}$
Hence, the correct option is (1).

question: The magnitude of axial field due to a bar magnet at a distance of  $1\,m,$   is found to be $5 \times 10^{-8} T$. The magnetic moment of the bar magnet is  $({\mu _0} = 4\pi  \times {10^{ - 7}}H/m)$
explanation:
Given, $r=1 m, B_{\text {axial }}=5 \times 10^{-8} T$
We know that, magnitude of axial field due to bar magnet.
$B_{\text {axial }}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{2 M}{r^{3}}$
$\Rightarrow 5 \times 10^{-8}=\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{4 \pi} \times \dfrac{2 M}{(1)^{3}}$
$\Rightarrow 5 \times 10^{-8}=2 \times 10^{-7} M$
$\Rightarrow M=\dfrac{5}{2} \times 10^{-1}=0.25 {~A}-{m}^{2}$
Hence, the correct option is (2).

question: Two short magnets of equal dipole moments $M$ are fastened perpendicularly at their centres which lies at origin. Let the magnets lie along $X$-axis and $Y$-axis, respectively. The magnitude of the magnetic field at a distance $R$ from the centre on the $Y$-axis is$\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{M_{0}}{R^{3}}$. Assuming, $R \gg l$ (magnet length), the magnitude of $M$ is
explanation:
Two magnet are joined as shown in figure. The magnetic field at point $P$ due to magnet $M_{1}$ is on axial line, while that due to $M_{2}$ is on equatorial line.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957945/preptracker/questions/q_111107_explanation_1764957944550.png
So, net magnetic field at a point $P$ at a distance $R$ on $Y$-axis,
${B_{{\text{net}}}} = \sqrt {B_1^2 + B_2^2} $ $ = \sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{2M}}{{{R^3}}}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{M}{{{R^3}}}} \right)}^2}} $
${B_{{\text{net}}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi {R^3}}} \cdot \sqrt 5 M\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
Given, ${B_{{\text{net}}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi {R^3}}} \cdot {M_0}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
By comparison Eq. (1) and Eq. (2), we get
$\sqrt{5} M=M_{0}$
$\Rightarrow M=\dfrac{M_{0}}{\sqrt{5}}$
Hence, the correct option is (3).

question: A bar magnet of magnetic moment $M$ is placed at a distance $D$ with its axis along positive $X$  - axis. Likewise, second bar magnet of magnetic moment $M$ is placed at a distance $2 D$ on positive $Y$ - axis and perpendicular to it as shown in the figure. The magnitude of magnetic field at the origin is $|B|=\alpha\left[\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{M}{D^{3}}\right]$. The value of $\alpha$ must be (assume $D \gg l$, where $l$ is the length of magnets)
explanation:
Magnetic field at axial point ( $d \gg 1$ )
$B_{1}=\dfrac{2 \mu_{0} M}{4 \pi d^{3}}$ (from $S$ to $N$ )
$=\dfrac{2 \mu_{0} M}{4 \pi D^{3}}(d=D)$
Magnetic field at equatorial line ( $d \gg 1$ )
$B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} M}{4 \pi d^{3}} \quad$ (from $N$ to $S$ )
Now, for equatorial point $d=2 D$
$\therefore \quad B_{2}=\dfrac{1}{8} \dfrac{\mu_{0} M}{4 \pi D^{3}} \quad$ (from $N$ to $S$ )
So, resultant magnetic field,
$\vec{B}=\vec{B}_{1}+\vec{B}_{2}\left(|\vec{B}|=\left|\vec{B}_{1}\right|-\left|\vec{B}_{2}\right|\right)$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0} M}{4 \pi D^{3}}\left(2-\dfrac{1}{8}\right)$
$B=\dfrac{15}{8} \dfrac{\mu_{0} M}{4 \pi D^{3}} \quad$ (in magnitude)
On comparing with given formula, we get $\alpha=15 / 8$
Hence, the correct option is (2).

question: Two identical bar magnets of magnetic moment $M$ each, are placed along $X$ and $Y$-axes, respectively at a distance $d$ from the origin (as shown in the figure). If the magnitude of total magnetic field at the origin is $B=\alpha\left[\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{M}{d^{3}}\right]$, then the value of constant $\alpha$ will be ( $d \gg l$, where $l$ is the length of the bar magnets and direction of $N$ to $S$ in magnets is opposite with respect to each other)
explanation:
Magnetic field due to axial magnet,
$B_{1}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{2 M}{d^{3}} \quad(+Y$ direction $)$
Magnetic field due to equatorial magnet,
$B_{2}=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{M}{d^{3}} \quad(+Y$ direction $)$
So, effective magnetic field
$\vec{B}=\vec{B}_{1}+\vec{B}_{2}=3 \dfrac{\mu_{0} M}{4 \pi d^{3}} \quad$ (along $Y$-direction)
$\Rightarrow \quad \alpha=3$

question: Two identical magnetic dipoles of magnetic moment $1.0\,A\,{m^2}$ each, placed at a separation of $2\,m$ with their axes perpendicular to each other. The resultant magnetic field at a point midway between the dipoles is
explanation:
The situation is shown in figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950733/preptracker/questions/q_43969_explanation_1764950732756.png The point $P$ at which the resultant magnetic field is to be determined is on the axial line of magnetic dipole $1$ and on the equatorial line of magnetic dipole $2.$
$\therefore \quad$ The magnetic induction at $P$ due to dipole
$1$ is
${B_1}{\rm{ }} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{2{M_1}}}{{r_1^3}} = \frac{{\left( {{{10}^{ - 7}}Tm{A^{ - 1}}} \right)(2)\left( {1A{m^2}} \right)}}{{{{(1\,m)}^3}}}$
$ = 2 \times {10^{ - 7}}\,T$
and that due to dipole 2 is
${B_2}{\rm{ }} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{{M_2}}}{{r_2^3}} = \frac{{\left( {{{10}^{ - 7}}Tm{A^{ - 1}}} \right)\left( {1A{m^2}} \right)}}{{{{(1\,m)}^3}}}$
$ = 1 \times {10^{ - 7}}\,T$
As $B_{1}$ and $B_{2}$ are perpendicular to each other
$\therefore$ The resultant magnetic field at $P$ is
$B = \sqrt {B_1^2 + B_2^2} = \sqrt {{{\left( {2 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}T} \right)}^2} + \left( {1 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}T} \right)} $
$\,\,\,\,\, = \sqrt 5 \times {10^{ - 7}}T$

question:
Assertion: The magnetism of magnet is due to the spin motion of electrons

Reason: Dipole moment of electron is smaller than that due to orbit motion around nucleus.
explanation:
The magnetism of a material is primarily due to the behavior of its electrons, specifically their intrinsic magnetic moments associated with their spin. When electrons spin on their axes, they generate magnetic dipole moments. The collective alignment of these dipole moments in a material is what gives rise to its magnetism.
In reality, the magnetic dipole moment associated with the spin of an electron is significantly greater than the dipole moment resulting from its orbital motion around the nucleus. The spin magnetic moment is an intrinsic property of electrons and is responsible for the majority of the magnetism in materials. The magnetic moment due to orbital motion is usually much smaller in comparison.
So, while the assertion is correct, the reason provided incorrectly states that the dipole moment due to orbit motion is smaller than that due to the spin motion, which is not the case. Therefore, the correct answer is (3) Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.

question: A magnet of magnetic moment $M$ is in the form of a quadrant of a circle. If it is straightened, its new magnetic moment will be
explanation:
Let $m$ be the pole strength of the magnet. Let $M$ and $M^{\prime}$ be the magnetic moments when the magnet is in arc shape and straight shape respectively. Let $\theta$ be the angle subtents by the arc at the centre. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950633/preptracker/questions/q_43733_explanation_1764950633525.png $M = m\,2R\sin \left( {\frac{\theta }{2}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$2 R \sin \left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$ is the effective length of the magnet.
The arc length of the magnet is $R \theta$. When the magnet is made straight then the magnetic moment is
$M' = ml = m\,R\,\theta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From eqns $1 \& 2$
$\dfrac{M}{M^{\prime}}=\dfrac{2 \sin \left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)}{\theta}$
Given that $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$
$\dfrac{M}{M^{\prime}}=\dfrac{2 R \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)}{\dfrac{\pi}{2}} \Rightarrow M^{\prime}=\dfrac{\pi M}{2 \sqrt{2}}$

question: The electron in a hydrogen atom is moving with a speed $2.3 \times {10^6}\,m{\rm{/}}s$ in an orbit of radius $0.5\,\mathop {{\rm{ }}A}\limits^{\,\,^\circ } $. Calculate the magnetic moment of revolving electron:
explanation:
Equivalent current
$I = \frac{e}{t} = \frac{e}{{\frac{{2\pi r}}{v}}} = \frac{{ev}}{{2\pi r}}$
$ = \frac{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 2.3 \times {{10}^6}}}{{2 \times 3.14 \times 0.53 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}$
$ = 1.105 \times {10^{ - 3}}A\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} M = IA = I\left( {\pi {r^2}} \right)$
$M = 1.105 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times 3.14 \times {\left( {0.53 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}} \right)^2}$
$M = 1.105 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times 3.14 \times 0.28 \times {10^{ - 20}}$
$M = 9.24 \times {10^{ - 24}}A{m^2}.$

question: Two identical north poles each of strength $m$ are kept at verticles $A$ and $B$ of an equilateral triangle $A B C$ of side a. The mutual force of repulsion between them has a magnitude of $F$. The magnitude of magnetic induction at $C$ is
explanation:
We know that $B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{m}{a^{2}}$,
Force between two poles is $F=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{m_{1} m_{2}}{a^{2}}$
$\begin{aligned}F_{r} & =\sqrt{F^{2}+F^{2}+2 F^{2} \cos 60^{\circ}} \\& =\sqrt{3} F=\sqrt{3} \dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{m^{2}}{d^{2}}=\sqrt{3} F\end{aligned}$
The net field at $C$ is $\Rightarrow B_{r}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3} F}{m}$.

question: Two identical bar magnets each of magnetic moment $M$, separated by some distance are kept perpendicular to each other. The magnetic induction at a point at the same distance $d$ from the centre of magnets, is ( $\mu_{0}=$ permeability of free space)

question: The ratio of magnetic fields due to a bar magnet at the two axial points $P_{1}$ and $P_{2}$ which are separated from each other by 10 $cm$ is $25: 2$. Magnetic length of the bar magnet is (points $P_{1}$ and $P_{2}$ are on the same side of magnet and distance of $P_{2}$ from the centre is greater than distance of $P_{1}$ from the centre of magnet)
explanation:
Let magentic field at $P_{1}$ is $B_{1}$ and at $P_{2}$ is $B_{2}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956429/preptracker/questions/q_43316_explanation_1764956428651.png $\therefore \dfrac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{x_{1}}{\left(x_{1}^{2}-l^{2}\right)^{2}}}{\dfrac{x_{2}}{\left(x_{2}^{2}-l^{2}\right)^{2}}}=\dfrac{\left(x_{2}^{2}-l^{2}\right)^{2}}{\left(x_{1}^{2}-l^{2}\right)^{2}} \times \dfrac{x_{1}}{x_{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{25}{2}=\dfrac{10}{20}\left[\dfrac{x_{2}^{2}-l^{2}}{x_{1}^{2}-l^{2}}\right]^{2}$
or $\left[\dfrac{x_{2}^{2}-l^{2}}{x_{1}^{2}-l^{2}}\right]^{2}=5$
or $x_{2}^{2}-l^{2}=5 x_{1}^{2}-5 l^{2}$
or $4 l^{2}=5 x_{1}^{2}-x_{2}^{2}$
$=5 \times(10)^{2}-(20)^{2}$
$=500-400=100$
$\Rightarrow l^{2}=25$
$ \Rightarrow l = 5\,cm$
Therefore, $2l = 10\,cm$

question: A steel wire of length $l$ has a magnetic moment $M$. It is then bent into a semicircular arc. The new magnetic moment is
explanation:

The diameter of semicircular wire,
$d=2 r=\dfrac{2 l}{\pi}$

${M^\prime } = m \times d = m \times \frac{{2l}}{\pi }\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
The magnetic moment of straight wire
$M=m l$
Substituting in eq.(1), we get
$M^{\prime}=\dfrac{2 M}{\pi}$

question: A non-conducting thin disc of radius $R$ rotates about its axis with an angular velocity $\omega$. The surface charge density on the disc varies with the distance $r$ from the centre as$\sigma(r)=\sigma_{0}\left[1+\left(\dfrac{r}{R}\right)^{\beta}\right]$, where $\sigma_{0}$ and $\beta$ are constants. If the magnetic induction at the center is $B=\left(\dfrac{9}{10}\right) \mu_{0} \sigma_{0} \omega R$, the value of $\beta$ is
explanation:
Total charge on the disc, $Q=\sigma\left(\pi R^{2}\right)$
Period of motion, $T=2 \pi / \omega$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952739/preptracker/questions/q_111125_explanation_1764952739485.png
So, current in the ring, $d I=\dfrac{d Q}{T}$
$\Rightarrow d Q=2 \sigma \pi r d r$
$\therefore \quad d I=\dfrac{2 \sigma \pi r d r}{2 \pi} \times \omega=\sigma \omega r d r$
Magnetic field due to ring is
$d B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} d I}{2 r}$
So, magnetic field due to disc is
$B=\int d B=\int_{0}^{R} \dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2 r} \cdot \sigma \omega r d r$
On putting value of $\sigma$, we get
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \sigma_{0} \omega}{2} \int_{0}^{R}\left[1+\left(\dfrac{r}{R}\right)^{\beta}\right] d r$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \sigma_{0} \omega}{2}\left[R+\dfrac{r^{\beta+1}}{R^{\beta}(1+\beta)}\right]_{0}^{R}$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \sigma_{0} \omega}{2} \cdot\left[R+\dfrac{R^{\beta+1}}{R^{\beta}(1+\beta)}\right]$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \sigma_{0} \omega}{2} \times\left(\dfrac{2+\beta}{1+\beta}\right) R$
Now, $B=\dfrac{9}{10} \mu_{0} \sigma_{0} \omega R=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \sigma_{0} \omega R}{2} \times \dfrac{(2+\beta)}{(1+\beta)}$
$\Rightarrow 5 \beta+10=9 \beta+9$
$\Rightarrow \quad \beta=\dfrac{1}{4}$
Hence, the correct option is (1).

question: A non-conducting disc of radius $R$ has surface charge density which varies with distance from the centre as $\sigma(r)=\sigma_{0}\left[1+\sqrt{\dfrac{r}{R}}\right]$, where $\sigma_{0}$ is a constant. The disc rotates about its axis with angular velocity $\omega$. If $B$ is the magnitude of magnetic field induction at the centre, then $\dfrac{B}{\mu_{0} \sigma_{0} \omega R}$ will be
explanation:
Consider an elemental ring of radius $r$ of thickness $d r$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952725/preptracker/questions/q_111124_explanation_1764952725421.png
Charge on ring, $d q=$ Area of ring $\times$ Charge density
$\Rightarrow d q=(2 \pi r d r) \sigma$
$\Rightarrow$ Current through ring, $d i=\dfrac{d q}{T}$, as angular velocity of disc is $\omega$.
So, $\quad \dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=T$
$\Rightarrow \quad d i=\dfrac{\omega d q}{2 \pi}=\dfrac{\omega(2 \pi r) \sigma}{2 \pi}=\omega r \sigma d r$
Now, magnetic induction due to elemental ring at the centre,
$d B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} d i}{2 r}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \omega r \sigma}{2 r} \cdot d r=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \omega \sigma}{2} \cdot d r$
As $\quad \sigma=\sigma_{0}\left(1+\sqrt{\dfrac{r}{R}}\right)$
For magnetic induction due to entire disc, we integrate from
$r=0$ to $r=R$
$\Rightarrow \quad B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \omega \sigma_{0}}{2} \int_{0}^{R}\left(1+\sqrt{\dfrac{r}{R}}\right) \cdot d r$
$=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \omega \sigma_{0}}{2}\left[r+\dfrac{r^{3 / 2}}{\dfrac{3}{2} R^{1 / 2}}\right]_{0}^{R}$
$\Rightarrow B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} \omega \sigma_{0}}{2}\left[R+\dfrac{2}{3} R\right]$
$\Rightarrow B=\dfrac{5}{6} \mu_{0} \omega \sigma_{0} R \Rightarrow \dfrac{B}{\mu_{0} \omega \sigma_{0} R}=\dfrac{5}{6}$
Hence, the correct option is (3).

Horizontal component of earth's magnetic field

$=200 \times 8000 \times 10^{-6}=1.6 {~A}-{m}^{2}$

question: Two tangent galvanometers $A$ and $B$ have coils of radii  $8{\text{ }}cm$  and  $16{\text{ }}cm$  respectively and having a resistance of $8 \Omega$ each. They are connected in parallel with a cell of emf    $4{\text{ }}V$  and negligible internal resistance. The deflections produced in the tangent galvanometers $A$ and $B$ are $30^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$, respectively. If $A$ has 2 turns, then $B$ must have
explanation:
The given situation is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952738/preptracker/questions/q_111109_explanation_1764952738031.png
Current through tangent galvanometer and deflection given by it are related as,
$i=\dfrac{2 r}{\mu_{0} N} \cdot B_{H} \cdot \tan \theta$
where, $r=$ radius of coil of galvanometer.
If $R=$ resistance of galvanometer, then potential drop across it is
$V=i R=\dfrac{2 r \cdot B_{H} \cdot \tan \theta \cdot R}{\mu_{0} \cdot N}$
In given case, $V_{A}=V_{B}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{2 r_{A} \tan \theta_{A}}{N_{A}}=\dfrac{2 r_{B} \tan \theta_{B}}{N_{B}}$
$\left[\because R_{A}=R_{B}=R=8 \Omega\right]$
$N_{B}=\dfrac{N_{A} \cdot r_{B} \cdot \tan \theta_{B}}{r_{A} \cdot \tan \theta_{A}}$
$=\dfrac{2 \times 16 \times 10^{-2} \times \tan 60^{\circ}}{8 \times 10^{-2} \times \tan 30^{\circ}}$
$=2 \times 2 \times 3=12$ turns
Hence, the correct option is (2).

question: A compass needle free to turn in a horizontal plane is placed at the centre of a circular coil of $20$ turns and radius $10\,cm$. explanation:
https://image.cleverb.in/upload/2024/12/7/1733550156409-73q.png
Here $n = 20,$$r = 10\,cm = 10 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m,$$i = 0.35\,A$,

${H}={B} \sin 45^{\circ}$

question: A long bar magnet has time period 2 $s$ in a vibration magnetometer. The magnet is cut along its length into 3 equal parts and the three parts are placed one over the other, like poles together. The time period of this combination will be :
explanation:
The time period of oscillations of magnet $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{I}{M H}\right)}$
where, ${I=}$ moment of inertia of magnet
${=\dfrac{m L^{2}}{12}}$( ${m}$ being the mass of magnet)
${M=}$ pole strength ${\times L}$
and ${H=}$ horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field.
When the three equal parts of magnet are placed on one another with their like poles together, then
${I^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{12}\left(\dfrac{m}{3}\right) \times\left(\dfrac{L}{3}\right)^{2} \times 3=\dfrac{1}{12} \dfrac{m L^{2}}{9}=\dfrac{I}{9}}$
and ${M^{\prime}=}$ pole strength ${\times \dfrac{L}{3} \times 3=M}$
Hence, ${T^{v}=2 \pi \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{I / 9}{M H}\right)} \Rightarrow T^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{3} \times T}$
${T=\dfrac{2}{3} {~s}}$.
So correct option is (2)

question: The reduction factor of a tangent galvanometer is ${K}$. If the number of turns and area of cross section of the coil are doubled, then the reduction factor becomes
explanation:

If ${N^{\prime} \rightarrow 2 N}$
Area ${A^{\prime} \rightarrow 2 A}$, $i.\,e.$ ${\pi r^{\prime 2}=2 \pi r^{2}}$
${\Rightarrow r^{\prime}=\sqrt{2 r^{2}} \Rightarrow r^{\prime}=\sqrt{2} r}$
${\therefore}$ New reduction factor becomes
${K^{\prime}=\dfrac{2 r^{\prime} B_{H}}{\mu_{0} N^{\prime}}}$
${\Rightarrow K^{\prime}=\dfrac{2 r \sqrt{2} B_{H}}{\mu_{0} 2 N} \Rightarrow K^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} K}$.
So correct option is (3)

question:
Assertion: If a compass needle be kept at magnetic north pole of the earth, the compass needle may stay in any direction.


Reason: Dip neelde will stay vertical at the north pole of the earth.
explanation:
At the magnetic North Pole of the Earth, the compass needle can point in any direction because the magnetic field lines converge at the North Pole. When a compass needle is placed there, it aligns itself with the local magnetic field direction, which can be in any direction as the lines converge. Therefore, the compass needle may stay in any direction. Assertion is true.
At the magnetic North Pole, the magnetic lines of force are nearly vertical (with a dip angle of approximately 90 degrees). In this situation, a dip needle, which is designed to respond to the angle of dip, will indeed point vertically upward. This is because the magnetic field lines are vertical at the North Pole, and a dip needle aligns itself with the local magnetic field lines.
However, the reason does not provide an explanation for the assertion. The assertion is about a standard compass needle, which is not a dip needle and does not respond to the angle of dip. The standard compass needle aligns itself with the horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field. Since the horizontal component is nearly zero at the North Pole, the compass needle can point in any direction, but this is not because of the dip angle; it's due to the convergence of magnetic field lines.
Therefore, the correct answer is (2)

question:
Which of the following statements is/are correct about magnetism?

II. When a bar magnet is freely suspended, it points in the North-South direction. The tip which points to the geographic North is called the North-pole and the tip which points to geographic South is called the South-pole of magnet.
III. There is a repulsive force when North-poles (or South-poles) of two magnets are brought close together. Conversely, there is an attractive force between the North-pole of one magnet and the South-pole of other.
IV. We can isolate the North or South-pole of a magnet.
Which of the following statements are correct?
explanation:
(I) The earth behaves as a magnet with the magnetic field pointing approximately from the geographic South to the North
(II) When a bar magnet is freely suspended, it points in the North-South direction. The tip which points to the geographic North is called the North pole and the tip which points to the geographic South is called the South-pole of the magnet.
(III) There is a repulsive force when North poles (or South-poles) of two magnets are brought close together. Conversely, there is an attractive force between the North-pole of one magnet and the South-pole of the other
(IV) We cannot isolate the North or South-pole of a magnet. If a bar magnet is broken into two halves, we get two similar bar magnets with somewhat weaker properties.

question: A 30$cm$ long bar magnet is placed in the magnetic meridian with its north pole pointing south. The neutral point is obtained at a distance of 40$cm$ from the centre of the magnet. Pole strength of the magnet is (The horizontal component of earth's magnetic field is 0.34 Gauss)
explanation:
As the magnet is placed with its north pole pointing south, the neutral points are obtained on the axial line. At the neutral points the magnetic field $B$ due to the magnet becomes equal and opposite to the horizontal component of earth's magnetic field i.e., $B_{H}$.
Hence, if $M$ be the magnetic dipole moment of the magnet of $2 \ell$ length and $r$ the distance of neutral point from the centre of the magnet, then we have
$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{2 M r}{\left(r^{2}-\ell^{2}\right)^{2}}=B_{H}$
Given that ${\mu _0} = 4\pi \times {10^{ - 7}}Tm{A^{ - 1}},$
$r = 40cm = 0.40m,\ell = 15cm = 0.15m$
${H_H} = 0.34$ Gauss $ = 0.34 \times {10^{ - 4}}T$
$\therefore M = \frac{{4\pi }}{{{\mu _0}}}.\frac{{{B_H}{{\left( {{r^2} - {\ell ^2}} \right)}^2}}}{{2r}}$
$ = {10^{ - 7}} \times \frac{{\left( {0.34 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}} \right) - {{\left[ {{{\left( {0.40} \right)}^2} - {{\left( {0.15} \right)}^2}} \right]}^2}}}{{2 \times 0.40}}$
$ = 8.0A{m^2}$
The pole strength of the magnet is,
$m = \frac{M}{{2\ell }} = \frac{{8.0}}{{0.30}} = 26.7Am$

question: Two tangent galvanometer $A$ and $B$ have coils of radius 8$cm$ and 16$cm$, respectively and resistance of $8 \Omega$ each. They are connected in parallel with a cell of emf 4$V$ and negligible internal resistance. The deflection produced in the tangent galvanometers $A$ and $B$ are $30^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$, respectively. If $A$ has 4 turns, then $B$ must have :
explanation:
Current in tangent galvanometer https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950406/preptracker/questions/q_42517_explanation_1764950406351.png $I=\dfrac{2 r H}{\mu_{0} N} \times \tan \theta$
Here, $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$ are in parallel
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \dfrac{1}{R_{n e t}}=\dfrac{1}{R_{1}}+\dfrac{1}{R_{2}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{R_{2}+R_{1}}{R_{1} R_{2}}=\dfrac{8+8}{8 \times 8} \\& \Rightarrow R_{n e t}=4 \Omega\end{aligned}$
Hence,$I = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{4}{4} = 1\,A$
From Eq. (1), we get
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{r \tan \theta}{N}=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 H}=\text { constant } \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{r_{A} \tan \theta_{A}}{N_{A}}=\dfrac{r_{B} \tan \theta_{B}}{N_{B}} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{8 \times 1}{\sqrt{3} \times 4}=\dfrac{16 \times \sqrt{3}}{N_{B}} \Rightarrow N_{B}=24 \text { turns. }\end{aligned}$

question: The earth's magnetic field at a certain place has a horizontal component of 0.3$G$ and total strength 0.5$G$. The angle of dip in $\delta$, then
explanation:
Horizontal component of earth's magnetic field is given by $B_{H}=B \cos \delta$
$\begin{aligned}& \text { or } \cos \delta=\dfrac{B_{H}}{B}=\dfrac{0.3}{0.5}=\dfrac{3}{5} \\& \therefore \dfrac{1}{\cos \theta}=\sec \delta=\dfrac{5}{3}\end{aligned}$
Now, $\tan \delta=\sqrt{\sec ^{2} \delta-1}=\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{5}{3}\right)^{2}-1}$
$=\sqrt{\dfrac{25}{9}-1}=\dfrac{4}{3} \Rightarrow \therefore \delta=\tan ^{-1} \dfrac{4}{3}$
